Ahilyabai Holkar

[Maratha History](https://sadashree.substack.com/s/maratha-history/?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=menu)

# Ahilyabai Holkar

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

May 31, 2025

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

8

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Share

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!vG04!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fa7ad60df-1281-4ea7-b66d-51a6fce62edd_1013x675.png)

![Why We Must Cherish The Legacy Of Devi Ahilyabai Holkar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!vG04!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fa7ad60df-1281-4ea7-b66d-51a6fce62edd_1013x675.png "Why We Must Cherish The Legacy Of Devi Ahilyabai Holkar")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

> *For thirty years her reign of peace,  
> The land in blessing did increase;  
> And she was blessed by every tongue,  
> By stern and gentle, old and young.  
> Yea, even the children at their mothers feet-Joanna Baillie*

The history of India has its own share of great women, throughout the ages who played a significant role as rulers, poets, warriors. Be it the Gond queen Rani Durgavati who defied the mighty Mughal army of Emperor Akbar, or the brave Rani Laxmibai of Jhansi who fought against the British their deeds were a part of folklore and history.

In such an illustrious pantheon, there was Ahilyabai Holkar, the daughter in law of Malhar Rao Holkar, the founder of the Holkar kingdom. Widowed at a young age, took over as queen, she takes her place in history among other great queens like Catherine II of Russia, Elizabeth I of England and Margaret I of Denmark. Known for her wisdom and administrative ability she rebuilt many Hindu temples, offered facilities for pilgrims, and built a new capital at Maheswar on the banks of the Narmada.

On the 31st of May, 1725, Ahilyabai was born in the Ahmednagar district of Maharashtra to a Dhangar village patil Malkoji Shinde. Though she did not go to school, nevertheless her father taught her to read and write at home. Fortune unexpectedly came her way, when the ruler of Malwa, Malharrao Holkar stopped at her village on his way to Pune and saw the 8 year old girl at a temple.

On that fateful day in 1733, Malharrao Holkar saw not just a child, but a spark—grace in her bearing, serenity in her gaze. He sensed something rare: a soul unshaken by circumstance, destined to shape history. Moved by her presence, he arranged her marriage to his son, Khanderao Holkar, thus weaving her into the fabric of the Holkar dynasty.

But fate, ever testing, struck cruelly.

The year was 1754. The air around Kumher was thick with smoke and sorrow. A cannonball had silenced Khande Rao Holkar, leaving Ahilyabai a widow at just 29. In the aftermath, tradition beckoned her toward Sati as she stood at the threshold, ready to follow her husband into death.

But Malharrao Holkar, her father-in-law, saw beyond ritual. He saw a kingdom teetering, a lineage broken, and a woman whose strength could rebuild what war had shattered.

*“You are not just my daughter-in-law,” he said. “You are Malwa’s hope.”*

With trembling hands, she stepped back from the fire—and into history

Malharrao Holkar didn’t just offer Ahilyabai a second chance—he gave her a mission. In the quiet corridors of the palace and the bustling courts of Malwa, he trained her in the art of rule: revenue systems, diplomacy, military strategy, and the delicate balance of justice.

She listened not as a student, but as a future sovereign.

*“You will not rule as a woman,” he told her. “You will rule as wisdom itself.”*

Ahilyabai absorbed every lesson, every nuance. She rode with the cavalry, studied legal codes, and learned to read the pulse of her people. When Malharrao passed away in 1766, she stepped into power—not as a placeholder, but as a force.

> *Proceed to Gwalior after crossing the Chambal. You may halt there for
> four or five days. You should keep your big artillery and arrange for
> its ammunition as much as possible….On the march you should arrange
> for military posts being located for protection of the road.*

An excerpt from a letter that illustrates Malhar Rao’s faith in Ahilyabai, in one of the most tumultuous period of Indian history.

Malharrao Holkar, her mentor and protector,passed away in 1766 and in 1767,Malerao Holkar, her son and successor, succumbed to illness and instability. The throne of Malwa stood vulnerable. Nobles whispered. Rivals stirred. Tradition loomed like a shadow.

But Ahilyabai, forged in fire and tutored in statecraft, did not flinch. She rode to the Peshwa court—not as a grieving mother, but as a prepared monarch. Her petition was bold:

*“I have been trained. I have governed. I have the trust of the army. Let me serve.”*

Some nobles balked. A woman ruler? A widow? But the Holkar army stood behind her, loyal not to her gender, but to her grit. The Peshwa, recognizing her capability and the stability she promised, granted her the reins of Malwa.

With the Peshwa’s seal of approval, Ahilyabai Holkar ascended the throne—not in silks and ceremony, but in armor and resolve. Her rule was not confined to the palace. When threats loomed, she mounted her elephant, armed with bow and arrow, and rode into battle.

She didn’t just command—she *led*. From the front.

*“Let my people see that their queen does not hide behind walls,” she once said. “Let my enemies know that dharma rides with me.”*

By her side stood **Tukojirao Holkar**, her commander-in-chief and trusted ally. Though not her biological son, he was her adopted heir in spirit—loyal, brave, and deeply respectful of her vision. Together, they formed a formidable duo, and ensured that Malwa remained peaceful, prosperous, and protected for decades.

With Tukojirao Holkar as her military advisor and confidant, Ahilyabai Holkar ruled Malwa not with pomp, but with purpose. Her court was not a fortress of privilege—it was a sanctuary of justice.

She refused to observe *purdah*, choosing instead to sit in open durbar, face uncovered, heart unguarded. Every morning, she held court—listening to farmers, merchants, widows, and warriors alike. Her throne was not elevated above the people; it was rooted among them.

*“A ruler must be seen,” she said. “Not as a deity, but as a servant of dharma.”*

Her wisdom was matched by her magnanimity. When a Brahmin who had once opposed her came seeking justice, she reinstated him without hesitation. Personal rivalry never clouded her judgment. She believed that forgiveness was not weakness—it was governance at its highest.

> *Her first principle of government appears to have been moderate
> assessment, and an almost sacred respect for the native rights of
> village officers and proprietors of land. She heard every complaint in
> person; and although she continually referred cases to courts of
> equity and arbitration, and to her ministers for settlement, she was
> always accessible. So strong was her sense of duty on all points
> connected with the distribution of justice, that she is represented as
> not only patient but unwearied in the investigation of the most
> insignificant cases, when appeals were made to her decision.” – Sir
> John Malcolm.*

The 18th century was a storm. Across Central India and Maharashtra, dynasties clashed, thrones changed hands, and blood soaked the soil. The Maratha Confederacy was riddled with internal strife. Rajputana saw endless skirmishes. Even Delhi trembled under invasions.

But in the heart of this turbulence, **Malwa remained untouched**—a tranquil kingdom ruled by a widow with no army of ambition, only an empire of empathy.

For 30 years, Ahilyabai Holkar governed with wisdom, restraint, and spiritual clarity. Her diplomacy was quiet but firm. Her military, under Tukojirao, was vigilant but never aggressive. Her people, protected and heard, had no reason to rebel.

*“Let others fight for thrones,” she said. “I shall fight for peace.”*

While Indore flourished as a bustling trading town under Ahilyabai’s reign—with caravans, artisans, and merchants weaving prosperity—her heart lay in **Maheshwar**, nestled on the sacred banks of the Narmada.

She chose Maheshwar not for its grandeur, but for its serenity. There, she built:

A majestic **fort and palace**, simple yet elegant.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!EkVM!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F61760664-5ca9-4bf9-8a56-410a6bc2c380_1024x682.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!EkVM!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F61760664-5ca9-4bf9-8a56-410a6bc2c380_1024x682.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Numerous **temples**, each a tribute to dharma and devotion.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!kEPb!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe8b8df04-969a-46a2-a31b-73694dd1b56b_275x183.png)

![Image](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!kEPb!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe8b8df04-969a-46a2-a31b-73694dd1b56b_275x183.png "Image")

Beautifully repaired **ghats**, where pilgrims could bathe and reflect.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!TGqi!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F225b374d-e063-4dad-9c2c-be3b2c75a555_275x183.png)

![Image](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!TGqi!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F225b374d-e063-4dad-9c2c-be3b2c75a555_275x183.png "Image")

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!MN1u!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F363fea8e-8292-4e6a-82c8-40e8742010f3_276x183.png)

![Image](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!MN1u!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F363fea8e-8292-4e6a-82c8-40e8742010f3_276x183.png "Image")

Roads and forts across Malwa, ensuring safety and connectivity.Generous donations to temples and sponsorship of **Hindu festivals**, reviving cultural life

In the quiet corridors of Maheshwar, where the Narmada whispered ancient hymns, Ahilyabai Holkar nurtured more than temples and trade—she nurtured *talent*. Her court welcomed voices that sang of dharma, minds that debated philosophy, and hands that wove heritage into fabric.

She patronized M**oropant**, the legendary Marathi poet whose verses echoed the spirit of Ramayana, **Anantapandhi**, the shahir whose ballads stirred hearts and preserved oral traditions and **Khushali Ram**, the Sanskrit scholar whose works enriched the intellectual life of her court.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!k6jX!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fd2f6a6c5-619f-4de8-80ff-7496a869c6dc_187x269.png)

![Image](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!k6jX!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fd2f6a6c5-619f-4de8-80ff-7496a869c6dc_187x269.png "Image")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

And beyond words, she uplifted **craft**. Under her reign, Maheshwar’s textile industry blossomed. The **Maheshwari sari**, with its delicate weave, vibrant colors, and reversible borders, became a symbol of elegance and identity—worn by queens and commoners alike.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!_XJz!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F3ecaecd3-d7cb-4b9d-b30c-9bdb84e35eb4_259x194.png)

![Image](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!_XJz!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F3ecaecd3-d7cb-4b9d-b30c-9bdb84e35eb4_259x194.png "Image")

Under Ahilyabai Holkar’s wise and inclusive rule, Malwa became more than a kingdom—it became a thriving ecosystem. She understood that true power lay not in palaces, but in the well-being of her people.

She encouraged trade and c**ommerce**, inviting merchants from across regions to settle and thrive. **Agricultural reforms**, ensuring fair taxation and irrigation support for farmers, **craft and cottage industries**, especially in textiles and pottery, **social mobility**, where cultivators and traders rose to affluence through merit and hard work.

Markets buzzed with activity. Fields yielded abundance. Artisans found patrons. Her policies were simple: low corruption, high transparency, and unwavering support for honest labor.

To Ahilyabai Holkar, her subjects were children under her care. Her governance was maternal, not imperial. She walked among the poor, listened to the voiceless, and gave dignity to the forgotten.

She invested heavily in **public works**,**tree-lined roads** to offer shade and beauty, **wells and tanks** to quench thirst and sustain agriculture,**rest houses (dharamshalas)** for weary travelers, **shelter for the homeless**, and aid for widows and orphans.

When **Bhil tribes** disrupted trade caravans, she didn’t send soldiers. She sent *herself*. She met them, listened to their grievances, and persuaded them to give up their nomadic raids. In return, she granted them **land for cultivation**, integrating them into the social fabric with respect.

“Let no one live in fear. Let every soul find a home,” she said.

Before the sun rose over Maheshwar, Ahilyabai Holkar was already awake—bathed in silence, wrapped in devotion. Her mornings began not with commands, but with *communion*. **An hour before daybreak**, she offered prayers to Lord Shiva, read **scriptures**, seeking wisdom before wielding power and distributed **alms to Brahmins**, honoring tradition with humility.

After a simple breakfast, she took a short pause—perhaps to reflect, perhaps to prepare. Then came her **daily durbar**, where she listened to her people, settled disputes, and made decisions with clarity and compassion.

Her governance was not separate from her faith—it flowed from it. A devout **Shaivaite**, she marked every royal proclamation with *“Shri Shankara”*, invoking Lord Shiva’s presence in every act of state.

Ahilyabai Holkar’s devotion was not confined to Maheshwar—it flowed across the subcontinent like the Narmada itself. She saw temple restoration not as royal duty, but as *spiritual healing*. Where invaders had razed sanctuaries, she raised them again—not in vengeance, but in veneration.

Her most iconic act of devotion re**building the Kashi Vishwanath Temple** in Varanasi, after it was destroyed by Aurangzeb. She didn’t just fund it—she revived its soul. **Ahilya Ghat** in Varanasi, a serene space named in her honor.

**Ahilya Dwarkeshwar Temple**, a testament to her reverence for Krishna.Repairs to the **Manikarnika** and **Dasasvamedh Ghats**, ensuring pilgrims could bathe and pray in dignity. Construction of **dharamshalas**, offering shelter to travelers and ascetics.

From Somnath to Rameswaram, from Gaya to Ayodhya, her patronage touched nearly every sacred corner of India.

*“Let every shrine rise again. Let every prayer find its home,” she believed.*

Her name became synonymous with sacred renewal. Even today, pilgrims whisper gratitude at the ghats she rebuilt.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!7tbK!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F11278bdc-96bc-4d24-9cf4-e3df5b8964be_251x360.png)

![Image](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!mPzb!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F540df9ca-d306-477f-bdef-a6615da80c54_275x183.png "Image")

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!A9-v!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F7cd757cb-fa20-42f4-a5a4-7306c711c27d_259x194.png)

![Image](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!A9-v!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F7cd757cb-fa20-42f4-a5a4-7306c711c27d_259x194.png "Image")

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!QilJ!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fea9bfa9f-3aea-4d19-b851-8492d11564a8_400x228.jpeg)

![Image result for Malhar Rao Holkar Chhatri](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!QilJ!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fea9bfa9f-3aea-4d19-b851-8492d11564a8_400x228.jpeg "Image result for Malhar Rao Holkar Chhatri")

> Malhar Rao Holkar Chhatri, Alampur, MP

Ahilyabai Holkar didn’t just rule Malwa—she *served Bharat*. Her devotion transcended geography, caste, and creed. From the icy sanctums of Badrinath to the coastal shrines of Rameswaram, her touch revived temples, repaired ghats, built dharamshalas, and sponsored poojas that kept the flame of dharma alive.

Her contributions include, **Treta Ram Temple** in Ayodhya, honoring Lord Rama’s legacy, **Chatri of Malharrao Holkar** at Alampur, along with several temples.

Restoration and patronage at pilgrimage sites like,**Badrinath**,**Dwarka** ,**Omkareshwar** ,**Puri**, Gaya, **Gaya** and **Rameswaram**. Every sacred site bore her imprint—whether through stone, shelter, or ceremony.

“Let no pilgrim walk alone. Let no shrine fall silent,” she believed.

On the morning of **13th August 1795**, the bells of Maheshwar rang not in celebration, but in mourning. Ahilyabai Holkar, the mother of Malwa, the guardian of dharma, had passed away. But her soul did not depart—it *dispersed*, like sacred ash across the rivers, temples, and hearts of India.

Walk into any major pilgrimage center in India—Varanasi, Ayodhya, Dwarka, Omkareshwar, Puri, Gaya—and you will find a whisper of her name, a stone laid by her hand, a shrine revived by her grace.

“She did not seek immortality. She built it.”

If you’re an Indori, you don’t just *know* Ahilyabai Holkar—you *feel* her. She’s in the air, the architecture, the rhythm of the city.

**Devi Ahilyabai Holkar Airport**, welcoming travelers with the dignity she once offered pilgrims. **Devi Ahilya Vishwavidyalaya**, a premier university nurturing minds in her name. **Rajwada Palace**, where she once ruled with grace and grit. Countless temples and ghats she built or restored, still echoing her devotion.

*“Ahilyabai was not just a queen,” Mahajan said. “She was Matoshree—a mother to all.”*

And her story continues to inspire. Former Lok Sabha Speaker **Sumitra Mahajan Tai**, herself an Indore MP, authored *Matoshree*—a dramatic retelling of Ahilyabai’s life, written over 35 years ago and later published as a Hindi play. It was even released by Prime Minister Narendra Modi, and staged in Parliament’s Balayogi Auditorium.
